name: Baalbek Lebanon
description: Baalbek, Lebanon, is home to one of the most impressive Roman temple complexes ever built. The site's history stretches back much further, however, with evidence of pre-Roman occupation. The massive stones used in the Temple of Jupiter, particularly the Trilithon (three stones weighing over 800 tons each), have fueled numerous alternative theories. Proponents suggest that the Trilithon, and perhaps other parts of the site, predate the Romans and were built by a more advanced, now-lost civilization or even extraterrestrial beings. They cite the immense size and precision of the stones, arguing that Roman technology was insufficient for such feats. Some also point to the lack of detailed historical records of the Trilithon's construction as evidence of a more mysterious origin. Mainstream archaeology attributes the construction of the Baalbek complex, including the Trilithon, to the Romans, albeit with possible earlier phases of construction or religious significance at the site. While the engineering feat is acknowledged as remarkable, scholars point to evidence of Roman quarrying techniques and unfinished stones as proof of Roman origin. They argue that the Romans possessed the necessary engineering knowledge and resources to move and place the stones, albeit with significant effort and ingenuity. The lack of detailed records is not considered unusual for the period, and the existence of unfinished stones suggests the project was interrupted or modified during its Roman phase. Alternative theories often disregard or downplay the Roman historical context and archaeological evidence, focusing instead on perceived anomalies and gaps in our understanding.
subtitle: Ancient mysteries of the Lebanese temple complex

claims.claim: The Trilithon stones predate Roman construction.
claims.evidence: Their immense size and precision are considered beyond Roman capabilities; lack of detailed Roman construction records.
claims.criticism: Mainstream archaeology attributes the stones to Roman engineering, citing evidence of Roman quarrying and unfinished stones.

claims.claim: Baalbek was built by a lost, advanced civilization.
claims.evidence: The advanced engineering required to move and place the Trilithon suggests a more sophisticated technology than the Romans possessed.
claims.criticism: No other archaeological evidence supports the existence of such a civilization in the region. Roman engineering was capable of moving large stones, albeit with significant effort.

claims.evidence: The sheer scale and precision of the stones are seen as evidence of technology beyond human capabilities, implying alien intervention.
claims.criticism: There is no scientific evidence to support extraterrestrial involvement in any ancient construction project. This claim relies on speculation and misinterpretation of archaeological evidence.

keyFigures.name: Erich von Däniken
keyFigures.role: Popularized the idea of extraterrestrial influence on ancient sites, including Baalbek.

keyFigures.name: Jeanine Abdul Massih
keyFigures.role: Lebanese archaeologist who studied Baalbek's history.

keyFigures.name: Theodor Wiegand
keyFigures.role: German archaeologist who conducted extensive excavations at Baalbek in the early 20th century.

timeline.event: Possible pre-Roman occupation and religious significance.
Roman construction of the Baalbek temple complex.
Increased interest in alternative theories about Baalbek's origins.

sources.author: Theodor Wiegand
sources.work: Baalbek: Ergebnisse der Ausgrabungen und Untersuchungen in den Jahren 1898 bis 1905
sources.title: Theodor Wiegand

sources.author: Talay, Caner
sources.work: Baalbek, Imagining a Roman Colony: Imperial Ideology and Local Realities
sources.title: Talay, Caner
